title: Bleeding a radiator
subtitle: Release trapped air, close when water appears
excerpt: How to bleed trapped air from a central heating radiator: recognising the signs of an air-locked radiator, using a bleed key, releasing the air safely, and restoring the system pressure afterwards.
sourceNotes: Standard UK domestic central heating practice. Boiler manufacturer guidance. CIPHE guidance on pressurised and gravity-fed heating systems.

recipeTools.notes: For the pressure-relief valve on the filling loop if needed.

content.text: Air trapped in a radiator prevents the hot water circulating to the top of the panel; the result is a radiator that is hot at the bottom and cold at the top. Bleeding releases the trapped air through the bleed valve at the top corner of the radiator. It takes two minutes once you have the bleed key; the rest is reading the boiler pressure gauge before and after.

Identify whether the system is sealed (pressurised, with a filling loop and a pressure gauge on the boiler) or open-vented (with a cold-water header tank in the loft). For a sealed system, note the boiler pressure before bleeding; it will drop slightly as air leaves and water is released, and may need topping up from the filling loop afterwards.

Allow the system to cool for at least 30 minutes so the water in the radiator is not under full operating pressure. The bleed valve opens a port directly into the hot water circuit; working with the heating running means more water under pressure escapes.

The bleed valve is a small brass nut with a square recess at the top corner of the radiator, usually at the side opposite the thermostatic radiator valve. Have the bleed key, a cloth, and a cup or small bowl ready before opening the valve.

Insert the bleed key into the square recess and turn anticlockwise by about a quarter turn. You will hear a hiss of escaping air. Hold the cloth below the valve to catch any drips. Keep the valve open until the air hiss stops and a steady bead of water appears. Then close the valve clockwise, hand-tight only. Do not overtighten; the bleed-valve threads are brass and soft.
Step 4, check and restore boiler pressure.

On a sealed system, check the pressure gauge on the boiler. It should read 1 to 1.5 bar when cold. If it reads below 0.8 bar, top up from the filling loop: slowly open both filling-loop valves until the pressure reads 1.2 bar, then close both valves. Do not overpressure; above 2 bar, the safety valve will release.
Step 5, restart the heating and check.

Switch the heating on and allow it to reach operating temperature. Check the previously cold radiator: it should now be uniformly hot from bottom to top. If it is still cold at the top, there may be more air, or the thermostatic radiator valve may need to be checked separately.
Water spraying when the valve is opened.
The system is still at full pressure; allow it to cool further before trying again, or reduce the boiler pressure slightly. Keep the cloth in place and close the valve immediately.

Persistent air in the system usually means the boiler is drawing in air from a low-pressure leak, or there is a pinhole corrosion leak in a radiator that is producing hydrogen gas. Have the system pressure-tested by a heating engineer.
